blocks.label: Table
blocks.html:
Birthday of Kurma.— See Tortoise Incarnation.
Birthday of Mārtand.— See Champā Shashthi.
Birthday of Narsingha.— See Narsingha Jayanti.
Birthday of Parashurām.— See Parashurām Jayanti.
Birthday of Rāma.— See Rāma Navami.

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Bodana—Curd, fried pulse-cakes called vada, etc., mixed up together and presented in oblation to Mahā-Lakshmi, or the tutelary goddess of a family, by a company of five married women. For folklore, see Mahā-Lakshmi.

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Bodhini—The eleventh day of the first half of Kārtik (October-November), when Vishṇu awakes from sleep. See Ekādashi for folklore.

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Brihaspati—The planet Jupiter, the Guru, or Preceptor, of the Gods. For folklore, see under Wednesday and Thursday Worships.

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Budh—The planet Mercury or Regent thereof. For folklore, see Wednesday Worship.

blocks.label: Text
blocks.html: Chaitra Gauri.—(March-April).—Gauri, or the wife of Shiva, is worshipped in this month by every married woman whose husband is alive. On the 3rd of the month a brass dola, or swing, containing the images of Shiva and his family, is placed on a raised dais, and the images are worshipped every morning. A fresh wreath of flowers has to be added every day. If flowers be not available, spun cotton is shaped like a wreath and put on. During the whole period, each family holds a ladies' meeting on selected days. All the married ladies and unmarried girls, that is, all women excluding widows, are invited to attend. The image of Gauri is dressed with clothes, and the best ornaments and jewellery are put on the figure. All the curiosities and nice things in the house are arranged in front of the
